body_txt: Whenever it is pitch dark, the characters' open eyes are always visible. The same applies, eventually, to any great big teeth which something lurking in the darkness may possess. A common shot is to have a nervous character scared in the dark as a variety of seemingly glowing teeth gather around them. In some cartoons, a character's eyes can just wander around independently from each other for maximum meta-humor. Due to being a visual stylization trope, this is by nature a native trope to drawn and animated media. In comics in particular, speech and thought bubbles and sound effects are also usually visible alongside "floating" eyes, due to also being medium abstractions rather than "real" things. A similar thing sometimes occurs in live-action, usually as ominous glinting eyes in darkness, but is visually distinct and usually used for spooky effect rather than as a shorthand way of telling watchers where a character is. Not to be confused with Glowing Eyes , where eyes explicitly glow in-universe rather than being a "reader-facing" stylization. The two tropes can overlap —robots and other characters of mechanical nature, for instance, can shine lights from their own optical sensors like headlights— but this trope frequently appears without the other, since in most cases the eyes aren't actually meant to be visible to the characters in the story like they are to the readers. A Sister Trope of The Darkness Gazes Back , where ominous glowing eyes populate dark scenes to frighten or menace characters; it's fairly common for these two tropes to be paired together when a character finds themselves lost in a dark area home to dangerous creatures. Super-Trope to Fade Around the Eyes . Compare and contrast Hollywood Darkness , where a scene is colored with a dark (usually blue) palette or put through a similar color filter as a way of telling the audience that it's dark and the characters can't see without preventing the audience from also not being able to tell what's going on. As a general rule, this trope tends to be used in shorter sequences for comedic effect and Hollywood Darkness in longer ones where impaired visibility would be more of a problem, but exceptions exist. Not to Be Confused with See the Whites of Their Eyes , which is a figurative expression for getting close to your enemy. Nor Eye Lights Out , which is a death trope.
